Patient ID O-3 underwent a surgical procedure consisting of a biopsy of the sentinel lymph node in the right axilla, a mastectomy of the right breast, and a mastectomy of the left breast.
The pathological report reveals that the sentinel lymph node biopsy was negative for any cancerous cells.
However, the right breast mastectomy showed invasive ductal carcinoma grade 3, measuring 4.5 cm in the largest dimension, with ductal carcinoma in situ grade 3 and necrosis.
The excision margins were negative.
The left breast mastectomy showed focal atypical ductal hyperplasia with negative margins.
Additionally, four lymph nodes from the right axilla were removed and were found to be negative for any signs of cancer.
The microscopic description provides further details about the histologic type, grade, architectural scores, and other characteristics of the tumors.
The report also mentions the presence of fibrocystic changes and apocrine metaplasia in the non-tumorous breast tissue.
Prognostic markers from a previous core biopsy are referenced for further evaluation.
The gross description provides information about the size and location of the tumors and lymph nodes.
Overall, the report confirms the presence of invasive ductal carcinoma in the right breast and provides additional details about the characteristics of the tumor and lymph nodes.
This pathological report is for a patient who underwent a right breast lumpectomy with full axillary node dissection.

The pre-operative diagnosis was right breast cancer, and the post-operative diagnosis confirmed the presence of poorly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma, measuring 7.5 cm in greatest dimension.
The tumor extends to the superior surgical margin near the skin.
There is also a high nuclear grade ductal carcinoma in-situ (DCIS) component with central necrosis and calcification present within less than 0.1 cm of the superior surgical margin.
The remaining designated surgical margins are free of tumor.
Ten regional lymph nodes in the axillary contents are positive for metastatic invasive ductal carcinoma.
The skin and attached fibrofatty breast tissue margins are negative for malignancy.
The pathological TNM stage is 3 N3 MX, stage IIIC, G3, invasive ductal carcinoma.
The patient's histologic score is 9 according to the Scarff-Bloom-Richardson breast cancer grading system.
The final diagnosis and staging indicate an aggressive form of breast cancer.
The patient's pathological report indicates that the right sentinel lymph node was negative for tumor.
The right breast segmental mastectomy revealed infiltrating duct carcinoma with a high grade (score 3) and no lymphovascular invasion.
The report also mentions the presence of ductal carcinoma in situ, solid type, with nuclear grades and comedo-type necrosis.
Approximately 3% of the invasive tumor was found in the surrounding breast tissue, and the closest margin was 0.09 cm.
The tumor is negative for progesterone receptors and HER2NEU.
The tumor is of basal-like type, with a Nottingham score of 9.
There is no dermal lymphatic invasion or calcification.
The surgical margins are not involved by the invasive or in situ component.
No lymph nodes were positive for metastasis out of the 1 examined.
The report does not mention the T, N, or M stage.
The estrogen receptors and HER2NEU are not specified.

The right axillary lymph node dissection specimen revealed that five out of sixteen lymph nodes were positive for metastatic carcinoma.

The patient underwent a modified radical mastectomy for a foGHT breast, which revealed multifocal infiltrating ductal carcinoma.
The largest tumor measured 1.5 cm and was located in the upper outer quadrant, while the second tumor measured 1.2 cm and was located at the junction of the upper outer and upper inner quadrants.
Both tumors were morphologically similar.
Lymphovascular invasion was extensive, but margins were negative.

Six out of sixteen axillary lymph nodes showed metastatic carcinoma with extracapsular extension.
The uninvolved breast showed fibrocystic changes and a fibroadenoma.
The nipple and skin were negative for neoplasm.
The report also noted calcific atherosclerosis and biopsy site changes.

Overall, this report indicates a case of multifocal infiltrating ductal carcinoma with lymph node involvement.

This is a pathological report for a patient who underwent a total organ resection of the left breast and axillary tissue.

The axillary lymph nodes showed metastases of carcinoma, with infiltration of the lymph node capsule and perinodal tissue.
The final histopathological diagnosis is invasive bifocal ductal carcinoma of the left breast with metastases in axillary lymph nodes.

The tumor is classified as pT 3, pN 1a (2/20), MX, RO, L1, V0. Further details have been redacted from the report.

They also had a history of right mastectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy, as well as left breast infiltrating ductal carcinoma with angiolymphatic invasion.
The pathological report shows extensive liver metastases, diffuse bilobar hepatic metastasis, pseudo cirrhosis, and ascites.

There is also involvement of the respiratory system, including congestion in both lungs, metastatic breast cancer with angiolymphatic invasion, bronchopneumonia, interstitial fibrosis, organizing pneumonia, and pulmonary fibrosis.
The report also mentions metastatic breast cancer involving the gastrointestinal system, with gastric mucosal congestion, petechial hemorrhages, and edema in the small bowel mucosa.

Other findings include metastatic breast cancer involving the right adrenal and perirenal fat, multifocal fat necrosis in the pancreas, metastatic breast cancer involving the endometrium, and diffuse osseous metastasis of breast cancer.
The probable cause of death is hepatic failure due to massive hepatic metastases, with metastatic breast cancer being an underlying cause.

The final diagnosis of the right axilla sentinel lymph node excision is metastatic ductal carcinoma measuring 0.9 cm with focal extracapsular extension.
The right breast, which underwent modified radical mastectomy, showed centrally located and involving tumor nodules ranging from 0.27 to 4.8 cm, with tumor aggregates up to 14.3 cm.
There was also carcinoma in situ of the solid type with comedo necrosis representing less than 5% of the tumor volume, as well as focal lobular carcinoma in situ.
Extensive lymphovascular space involvement was observed, and the tumor was found to be 0.8 cm from the lower inner quadrant deep margin.
Margins were free of tumor.
The skin and nipple showed dermal lymphovascular space involvement, and changes consistent with the biopsy site were noted.
The left breast, which underwent breast reduction, showed fibrocystic changes with microcalcifications, but the skin was unremarkable.
There were fourteen lymph nodes in the right axilla, all of which were negative for tumor.
The primary invasive carcinoma of the breast was centrally subareolar in location, with a maximum dimension of 4.8 cm.
Multicentricity and multifocality of invasive foci were present, with a tumor aggregate size of 14.3 cm.
The tumor type was ductal adenocarcinoma, and the Nottingham score was 6 out of 9 (tubules 3, nuclei 2, mitoses 4).
Angiolymphatic invasion and dermal lymphatic invasion were observed, and there were calcifications present.
In situ tumor types included comedo-type ductal carcinoma in situ and focal lobular carcinoma in situ.
The surgical margins were not involved by invasive carcinoma, but the surgical margins were involved by in situ carcinoma.
There was no evidence of diseases involving the nipple.
The lymph node examination revealed sentinel node metastasis with only keratin-positive cells present, and the size of nodal metastases was not mentioned.
There was no extracapsular extension or ulceration.
The pathological stage was not mentioned.
Information regarding estrogen receptors, progesterone receptors, and HER2NEU status was not provided.
Summary:.
The pathological report describes the findings from a histopathological examination of a removed tumor from a female patient.
The tumor was located in the upper outer quadrant of the breast and measured 12.2 x 4.9 x 2.4 cm.
Microscopic examination revealed invasive mucinous carcinoma with some ductal features, as well as ductal carcinoma in situ.
There were also lesions of intraductal carcinoma and fibrocystic changes in the glandular tissue.
No lymph node involvement was observed.
The tumor was found to be positive for estrogen and progesterone receptors, but negative for HER2 protein.
The final diagnosis is invasive mucinous and ductal carcinoma with ductal carcinoma in situ, stage T2, with no lymph node involvement.
The report suggests contacting the doctor for further discussion.
Summary:.
The patient, a female of unspecified age and race, underwent a right breast mastectomy.
The tumor was identified as infiltrating ductal carcinoma, no special type, with moderately differentiated features.
The tumor size was measured at 3.0 cm, with extensive venous/lymphatic invasion.
The margins were negative, and there was intraductal carcinoma in situ present.
The lymph nodes in the right axilla were also examined, with one sentinel node and one non-sentinel node testing positive for metastatic carcinoma.
Extranodal extension by carcinoma was observed in one lymph node.
The estrogen and progesterone receptors were positive, while HER2 was negative.
The final pathologic stage was pT2 Nia Mx.
Additional pathologic changes included fibrocystic changes, adenosis, ductal hyperplasia, fibroadenomatoid change, and columnar cell change without atypia.
The pathological report indicates that the patient, Caramenn, underwent a left breast wide local excision procedure for a 1.2 cm tumor located 1 cm from the closest/posterior margin.

The gross examination of the specimen revealed invasive ductal carcinoma with a SBR grade 1. Additionally, there was ductal carcinoma in situ of the cribriform type and ductal hyperplasia involving an intraductal papilloma.
Four sentinel lymph nodes were biopsied during the surgery.
One lymph node (sentinel #1) showed metastatic carcinoma measuring 0.5 mm (micrometastases) without extranodal extension.
Another lymph node (sentinel #2) also had metastatic carcinoma measuring 0.6 mm (micrometastases) without extranodal extension.
The third sentinel lymph node (sentinel #3) was negative for metastases.
The fourth sentinel lymph node (sentinel #4) showed metastatic carcinoma measuring 2.0 mm (micrometastases) without extranodal extension.
The report mentions the presence of two foci of intraductal papilloma with usual ductal hyperplasia, one adjacent to the invasive tumor and one at the posterior surgical resection margin.
The marker proteins p63 and calponin were used to identify these foci.
This pathological report describes a case of primary invasive carcinoma of the breast in a patient.
The tumor is located in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast and measures 1.3 cm in its maximum dimension.
It is classified as ductal adenocarcinoma, with a Nottingham grade of 3.
The tubule score is 3, nuclear grade is 3, and the mitotic activity score is also 3, resulting in a total Nottingham score of 9.
There is no evidence of angiolymphatic invasion or lymphatic invasion.
Surgical margins are not involved by the invasive component, with a distance of 8 mm from the tumor to the closest margin.
Two lymph nodes were examined and showed no metastasis.
The hormone receptor status (estrogen and progesterone receptors) and HER2 expression are unknown.
Other findings in the biopsy include fibrocystic changes, columnar cell change, and pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia (PASH).

The pathological report indicates that the patient underwent a HER2 gene amplification test using dual in situ hybridization.
The results showed that the patient is negative for HER2 gene amplification.
The test was performed as a reflex test after the HER2/NEU immunostain was scored as 2+.
The specimen used for the test was a formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tissue slide.
The assay allows for co-hybridization and visualization of the HER2 gene and Chr17 centromere on the same slide.
The HER2 gene is detected using a dinitrophenyl labeled probe, while the Chr17 centromere is targeted with a digoxigenin labeled probe.
Slide scoring was performed, and if the HER2/Chr17 ratio falls within 1.8-2.2, additional nuclei scoring is recommended.
The HER2 gene status is reported as non-amplified if the ratio is less than 2.0, or amplified if the ratio is greater than 2.0..
In addition, immunohistochemical evaluation was performed on the tissue for estrogen receptors, progesterone receptors, and HER-2NEU in invasive mammary carcinoma.
The results showed 99% positivity for estrogen receptors and 40% positivity for progesterone receptors.
The staining intensity for both receptors was reported as moderate.
HER-2NEU scoring was equivocal with a score of 2+.
The immunohistochemical studies used monoclonal antibodies and control sections for proper evaluation.

The reactivity of estrogen and progesterone receptors was determined based on the percentage of positive cells, with reference values indicating positivity if nuclear staining is present in 1% or more of invasive carcinoma cells.
The staining intensity for HER-2NEU was evaluated according to CAP scoring guidelines.

There was also ductal carcinoma in situ with high nuclear grade and necrosis.
The surgical margins were clear of invasive carcinoma.
Sentinel lymph nodes were examined, and one lymph node was found to have isolated tumor cells, while the remaining lymph nodes were negative for carcinoma.

The pathology report also includes information about the size and grade of the tumor, as well as the absence of lymph-vascular invasion and dermal lymph-vascular invasion.

Based on the available information, the pathologic staging of the tumor is pT2 and pNO(i+), indicating a tumor larger than 20 mm but less than or equal to 50 mm in greatest dimension and the presence of malignant cells in regional lymph nodes no greater than 0.2 mm.
Ancillary studies were performed on another specimen, which confirmed the presence of estrogen receptors in more than 30% of tumor cells.
The report does not indicate the results of progesterone receptor testing.
Overall, the pathology report provides important information about the extent and characteristics of the breast cancer, as well as the involvement of lymph nodes.
This information is necessary for determining appropriate treatment options and prognosis for the patient.

FINAL DIAGNOSIS:.
The pathological report reveals the following findings:.
1. Left axillary sentinel lymph node biopsy: One lymph node was examined and found to be negative for tumor.
2. Invasive carcinoma: The tumor measures 1.8 cm in greatest dimension.
It is a ductal carcinoma, Nottingham grade 3, with characteristics including tubule formation, nuclear grade, and mitotic rate.
The total score is 9/9.
3. Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS): DCIS is present and classified as nuclear grade 3 with solid and micropapillary types.
It also shows comedo necrosis and associated calcifications.
The DCIS is located outside of the invasive carcinoma component.
4. Resection margins: The margins of resection are negative for invasive carcinoma.
The invasive carcinoma is located 0.9 cm from the closest (anterior) margin of resection.
However, DCIS is present at the inferior margin of resection.
5. Lymphovascular invasion: No lymphovascular invasion is identified in the current specimen.
Previous reports also indicate the absence of lymphatic invasion.
6. Other findings: Atypical ductal hyperplasia, fibrocystic changes with columnar cell change, and ductal epithelial hyperplasia were observed.
The invasive carcinoma is negative for estrogen and progesterone receptors and negative for HER-2/NEU.
Additional comments state that the in situ carcinoma is located near the inferior margin and involves the anterior margin in three slides.
The entire specimen was evaluated histologically.
There is uncertainty about lymphatic invasion due to limited information.

1. Invasive ductal carcinoma: The tumor measures 2.3 cm and has a moderately differentiated grade (Nottingham score 6/9).
It is accompanied by ductal carcinoma in situ (5% of tumor volume) with cribriform type and associated comedo necrosis.
2. Lymphovascular invasion: No definitive evidence of lymphovascular invasion is observed.
3. Margins: The invasive carcinoma extends to the superior margin, while the remainder of the margins is free.

4. Other findings: Atypical ductal hyperplasia, flat epithelial atypia, fibrocystic changes with calcifications, ductal epithelial hyperplasia, fibroadenomatoio nodule, and previous biopsy site changes are present.
5. Hormone receptor status: The tumor is estrogen receptor-positive (ER-positive) and progesterone receptor-positive (PR-positive) but HER2-negative.
6. Sentinel lymph node biopsy: One lymph node shows 1 mm of micro metastatic carcinoma without extra capsular extension.
7. Non-sentinel lymph node biopsy: One benign lymph node is found, without any tumor cells.
Based on these findings, the patient has a moderate-grade invasive ductal carcinoma with associated in situ component.
The tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative.
There are micro metastatic carcinoma cells in one sentinel lymph node but no involvement in the non-sentinel lymph node.

In the right axillary lymph node dissection, multiple lymph nodes (18) were negative for tumor.
The left breast also underwent a total mastectomy, and the pathology showed ductal carcinoma in situ of the micropapillary and cribriform types.
It had a low nuclear grade without intraluminal necrosis.
The area involved measured 5.7 x 2.3 x 1.4 cm.
Similar to the right breast, nonproliferative fibrocystic changes were seen in the non-neoplastic breast parenchyma.
Calcifications were present in malignant ducts and biopsy site changes were noted.
The nipple and skin did not show any abnormality.
All surgical resection margins were negative for tumor.
Further evaluation and treatment will be based on these findings.

Pathological report for a patient:.
The patient underwent a mastectomy for a tumor in the right breast.
The tumor was identified as infiltrating ductal carcinoma, poorly differentiated with a Nottingham grade of 3.
The tumor measured 4.5 cm in its greatest dimension and showed necrosis.
Microcalcifications were present in the tumor as well as venous and lymphatic invasion.
Margins were positive for tumor involvement and there was minimal intraductal involvement.
Nipple and skin involvement were also present.
Multicentricity was observed with multiple microscopic foci in other quadrants.
Estrogen and progesterone receptors were positive, and HER2 Neu was weakly positive by IHC, with pending confirmation by FISH.
Lymph node dissection revealed eight lymph nodes positive for tumor, with extensive extracapsular extension.
The patient's previous material collected by mammotome biopsy was tested for ER, PR, and HER2 Neu and will not be repeated.
Satellite nodules were observed throughout all quadrants, likely due to intramammary lymphatic spread.
The degree of extranodal extension could not be accurately measured due to extensive perinodal tumor deposits.
The final pathologic stage was pT4d N2a MX.
In addition to the carcinoma, there was also high-grade ductal carcinoma in situ and cystic change with apocrine metaplasia.

Pathological Report Summary:.
The patient underwent a left breast mastectomy, and the final diagnosis is moderately differentiated infiltrating lobular carcinoma with a tumor size of 3.5 cm.
There is no tumor necrosis or venous/lymphatic invasion identified, and the margins are negative.
The tumor is estrogen receptor positive, progesterone receptor negative, and weakly positive for HER2/NEU.
The pathologic stage is pT2 NO MX.
Additional pathologic changes include usual ductal hyperplasia, fibroadenomatoid change, fibrocystic changes, and prior biopsy site changes.
Microscopic foci of infiltrating lobular carcinoma and lobular carcinoma in situ are also present.

The foci likely represent extension of the main tumor rather than true multifocal carcinoma.

The overall diagnosis is invasive lobular carcinoma of the left breast, with the presence of metastases in the axillary lymph nodes.

The pathological report describes the findings from a histopathological examination of a resected left breast and axillary tissues.
The examination revealed a carcinoma lobular invashvum with invasive lesions present.
Estrogen receptors were found in 75% of neoplastic cell nuclei, while progesterone receptors were found in 10%.
The HER2 protein stained positive but further verification using the FISH method was recommended.
Macroscopically, the left breast measured 17.8 x 11.3 x 4.8 cm and weighed 400g.
The microscopic examination confirmed the diagnosis of invasive lobular carcinoma of the left breast with metastases in axillary lymph nodes.
The HER2/neu gene amplification was not found using the FISH method.
The pathological report describes the findings from a primary breast surgery.
The patient had invasive lobular carcinoma in the right breast, with focal duct carcinoma in-situ.
The tumor had a size of 3.0 cm and was associated with extensive duct carcinoma in-situ.
The tumor was of intermediate nuclear grade and solid type with comedo necrosis.
There was no lymphatic invasion identified.
Other findings included intraductal papilloma, radial scar, fibrocystic changes, and calcifications in the vessel wall.
The axillary lymph nodes showed metastatic carcinoma in two out of thirteen nodes.
The HER-2-NEU oncoprotein stain was negative.
Further details of the tumor characteristics and hormone receptor status are mentioned in an addendum.
The overall stage of the disease was pT2N1aMx.
The pathological report indicates that the patient had a simple mastectomy on their right breast due to multiple foci (4) of infiltrating lobular carcinoma.
The carcinoma is classified as Nottingham grade I and formed a bilobed mass in the 9:30 region of the breast, as well as smaller masses in other regions.
The smaller masses are associated with extensive lobular carcinoma in situ.
There is no angiolymphatic invasion, and the surrounding breast tissue shows proliferative fibrocystic changes.
Calcifications are present in benign ducts and acini.
The biopsy site shows changes, but the surgical margins are negative for tumor.
One of the three axillary sentinel lymph nodes that were removed is positive for micrometastasis, measuring 0.6 mm.
There is no extranodal extension.
A nevus is present in the skin of the right chest wall.
